title: AviList
focus: Unified global checklist of bird species for conservation.
rationale: A standalone initiative for bird conservation replacing older international lists.
sectionExcerpt: AviList: The first-ever unified global checklist of bird species to aid effective bird conservation. Collaborative global effort that included representatives from BirdLife International, the Cornell Lab of Ornithology... It will replace the International Ornithological Committee (IOC) and Clements lists and will be updated annually.
evidenceBullets: AviList is the first-ever unified global checklist of bird species.
It replaces the International Ornithological Committee (IOC) and Clements lists.
It is a collaborative effort involving BirdLife International and the Cornell Lab of Ornithology.

title: Conservation Reserves and Hesaraghatta Grassland
focus: Legal status of Conservation Reserves under WPA 1972 and details of Hesaraghatta Grassland.
rationale: Combines the statutory framework of Conservation Reserves with the specific geographical and ecological details of Hesaraghatta Grassland.
sectionExcerpt: State Government has notified Hesaraghatta Grassland Conservation Reserve under Section 36A of the Wildlife Protection Act (WPA) 1972. Considered a protected area under WPA 1972 and can be declared by State Governments and Central Government... Typically act as buffer zones to or connectors and migration corridors... Hesaraghatta Grassland Location: Around Hesaraghatta Lake near Bengaluru in Karnataka and is the last remaining grassland habitat in Bengaluru region. Large catchment of the Arkavathy River.
evidenceBullets: Conservation Reserves are declared under Section 36A of the Wildlife Protection Act (WPA) 1972.
They act as buffer zones or migration corridors between established national parks, sanctuaries, and forests.
A Conservation Reserve Management Committee advises the Chief Wild Life Warden.
Hesaraghatta Grassland is the last remaining grassland habitat in the Bengaluru region.
It forms a large catchment for the Arkavathy River and Thippagondanahalli reservoir.

title: Global Ocean Darkening
focus: Causes, mechanisms, and impacts of reduced light penetration in global oceans.
rationale: A specific environmental phenomenon with distinct causes (coastal vs open ocean) and ecological impacts.
sectionExcerpt: A study found that 21% of the global ocean had become darker between 2003 and 2022, especially in the Arctic, Antarctic, and Gulf Stream region. It refers to a reduction in light penetration into the global oceans, shrinking the photic zone. Reasons: In Coastal Oceans: Combination of nutrient, organic material and sediment loading... In Open Oceans: warming of the surface oceans (leading to algal bloom)... Impact: Limits Ocean productivity.
evidenceBullets: 21% of the global ocean became darker between 2003 and 2022, notably in the Arctic, Antarctic, and Gulf Stream.
Ocean darkening shrinks the photic zone, which is the sunlit layer up to 200 meters deep where 90% of marine life thrives.
Coastal darkening is caused by nutrient, organic material, and sediment loading from agricultural runoff.
Open ocean darkening is driven by surface warming leading to algal blooms and climate-driven circulation changes.
Impacts include limited ocean productivity, declining fish stocks, and affected climate regulation.

title: Environmental DNA (eDNA)
focus: Definition, applications, and significance of eDNA in biodiversity conservation.
rationale: A modern biomonitoring tool with specific applications and advantages over traditional methods.
sectionExcerpt: Scientists are now focusing on modern tools like environmental DNA (eDNA) analysis for biodiversity conservation. It is the genetic material, like DNA from cells, tissues, fluids, and excrement, left by organisms in the environment (e.g., water, soil, Air, etc.). Application: Mapping species presence and distribution, tracking Ecosystems, etc. Significance: Non-invasive, highly sensitive, rapid and cost-effective.
evidenceBullets: eDNA is genetic material left by organisms in environments like water, soil, or air.
It is used for mapping species presence, distribution, and tracking ecosystems.
It is a non-invasive, highly sensitive, rapid, and cost-effective biomonitoring method compared to traditional methods.

title: Blue Washing and Related Terms
focus: Deceptive marketing tactics including blue washing, greenwashing, and pinkwashing.
rationale: Groups related corporate deceptive marketing terminologies relevant to environmental and social ethics.
sectionExcerpt: To counter 'greenwashing' allegations against it, Waste to energy (WTE) industry seems to have adapted 'blue washing'. About Blue Washing: It is a deceptive marketing tactic that overstates a company's commitment to responsible and sustainable social and ethical business practices. Greenwashing: Making false or misleading claims about a company's environmental efforts... Pinkwashing: Using LGBTQ+ rights as a marketing tool.
evidenceBullets: Blue washing overstates a company's commitment to responsible, sustainable, social, and ethical business practices.
Greenwashing involves making false or misleading claims about environmental efforts to appear eco-friendly.
Pinkwashing uses LGBTQ+ rights as a marketing tool while failing to improve actual conditions for LGBTQ+ employees.

title: Functional De-extinction
focus: Concept, techniques, and examples of reviving ecological roles of extinct species.
rationale: A distinct biotechnology and conservation concept involving genetic engineering to mimic extinct species.
sectionExcerpt: A US biotech company, Colossal Biosciences, claims to have genetically engineered three grey wolf pups to carry traits of the extinct dire wolf, calling it world's first successful functional de-extinction. It focuses on reviving ecological roles and traits of extinct species. Revived organisms are not exact replicas but genetically engineered to closely resemble extinct species... Techniques used: CRISPR gene editing, cloning, and genome mapping.
evidenceBullets: Functional de-extinction focuses on reviving the ecological roles and traits of extinct species rather than creating exact replicas.
Techniques include CRISPR gene editing, cloning, and genome mapping.
Colossal Biosciences engineered grey wolf pups to carry traits of the extinct dire wolf.
Other planned candidates for functional de-extinction include the wooly mammoth, moa, thylacine, and dodo.

title: Ungulates
focus: Characteristics, habitat, and ecological significance of hoofed mammals in India.
rationale: Covers a specific animal group surveyed by WII and NTCA, detailing their ecological role.
sectionExcerpt: Wildlife Institute of India and National Tiger Conservation Authority did India's first survey of ungulates (hoofed animals), warning of their decline in Odisha, Jharkhand, and Chhattisgarh. Characteristics: Primarily herbivores mammals including chital, antelopes, sambar, nilgai, etc.; Prey species for tigers. Significance: Aid forest/grassland regeneration through seed dispersal; nutrient cycling; soil health.
evidenceBullets: Ungulates are hoofed mammals, primarily herbivores, including chital, antelopes, sambar, and nilgai.
WII and NTCA conducted India's first survey of ungulates, noting declines in Odisha, Jharkhand, and Chhattisgarh.
They serve as key prey species for tigers.
They aid in forest and grassland regeneration through seed dispersal, nutrient cycling, and maintaining soil health.
